title: Sedona
dek: Six nights among the red rocks, built around a slow pace, a real spa day, and food that isn't the Uptown tourist strip — a first solo trip with room to breathe. Everything below is checked; tick items off as you go and your progress saves.
verified: Checked 19 Jul 2026 for the 2–8 Sep trip. Confirmed from primary sources: TSA REAL ID/passport rules (tsa.gov), the Red Rock Pass program and prices (fs.usda.gov, official Coconino National Forest), Sedona Shuttle's live route status (sedonashuttle.com — 2 of 3 free trailhead routes suspended), the Pocket Fire closure order and Oak Creek flooding closure (fs.usda.gov/r03/coconino/alerts, Forest Orders #03-04-00-26-004 and #03-04-06-26-002 — active through the trip window), Mii amo's current Journey package structure (miiamo.com — 2/3/4/7/10-night, not day treatments), Tlaquepaque's own hours (tlaq.com family of sources), local food picks cross-checked across multiple local guides, and Sedona/Cathedral Rock/Bell Rock/Airport Mesa/Tlaquepaque/Uptown/Phoenix coordinates (Nominatim). Time zone (America/Phoenix — Arizona observes no DST) resolved from Sedona's own coordinates via scripts/lookup-tz.mjs, not assumed from the country. The budget line was re-checked against its own numbers: the full week (including the Day 2 spa splurge) runs ≈$327/day, about 9% over the stated $150–300/day target, not just "approaching" it. ⚠ re-check before you go: exact hotel and rental-car rates (both move week to week), L'Auberge de Sedona's current spa-treatment prices (not published online — call to book), whether the Pocket Fire closure order (through Sep 30, unless rescinded early) or the Oak Creek flooding closure have lifted, the Sedona Shuttle's route status again close to your dates, and the NWS flash-flood/monsoon forecast in the days immediately before you go.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Six nights based in Sedona — a slow, single-base trip built around a spa day, real local food away from the Uptown strip, and the red rocks at an easy pace rather than a strenuous one. September falls squarely inside Arizona's monsoon season (mid-June through end of September) — afternoon thunderstorms and flash-flood risk are real considerations here, not a footnote, so outdoor time is planned for mornings. A rental car is the practical way to get around: rideshare coverage is inconsistent, and two of Sedona's three free trailhead shuttle routes are suspended for the Pocket Fire closures north of town (through Sep 30 — see Health & safety) — neither affects this trip's main hiking days, both south of the fire area.

sections.title: When you land
sections.body:
The realistic gateway is Phoenix Sky Harbor (PHX) — Sedona has no meaningful commercial airport of its own. From PHX it's ≈116 miles / about 2 hours by car via I-17 and AZ-179 (add 30 minutes for weekend/holiday traffic). Pick up a rental car at PHX rather than relying on rideshare once you're in Sedona (see Getting around). If you'd rather not drive the mountain road yourself, a door-to-door airport shuttle (Groome Transportation and others) runs ≈$55–100 per person, taking ≈2h45m. Flagstaff Pulliam is a smaller, closer alternative (≈30 min to Sedona) if it happens to serve your home airport — check before assuming PHX is your only option.

Budget this whole first day as travel — arriving in Sedona tired from a flight-plus-drive is normal, not a sign anything went wrong.

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: English is the primary language. Same electrical plugs and voltage as anywhere else in the US — no adapter needed. Driving is on the right. Tipping follows standard US norms: 18–20% at sit-down restaurants, $1–2/bag for hotel bellhops, $5–10/day for housekeeping. Sedona sits at about 4,350 ft elevation — noticeably higher and drier than most US cities; mild altitude effects (thirst, fatigue) are common for the first day or two.

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: This is a domestic US destination — Arizona is a US state, so a US citizen needs no passport or visa. At the TSA checkpoint for the flight itself: a REAL ID–compliant driver's license or another TSA-accepted photo ID (a US passport always works as a substitute). Without an acceptable ID, TSA's ConfirmID process costs $45 as of 1 Feb 2026. ⚠ If flying in from outside the US, entry rules depend on your own passport's visa status for the United States generally — this guide's intake left home country blank, so that check wasn't done here.

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body: Sedona runs on the same major US carriers as the rest of the country — if your plan covers domestic US roaming, it works here with no changes. Coverage is solid in town and along the main highways; it thins out in the canyons and at the more remote trailheads.

sections.title: Booking checklist
sections.body: Sedona lodging runs on the pricier side for a US destination (see Budget below) — book early, and confirm AC even though this is high desert, not tropical, since September afternoons still reach the high 80s°F.
checklist: Flights into PHX (or Flagstaff, if it serves your route)
Rental car — book ahead, prices rise closer to the date
Spa appointment (Day 2) — call L'Auberge or check Mii amo's package availability
Red Rock Pass — buy the 7-day pass online or at a visitor center before your first trailhead stop

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body: US Dollar, no currency exchange needed. Cards are accepted almost everywhere in Sedona, including the local food spots below — this is not a cash-only town the way some destinations are. Tipping norms match the rest of the US (see Local essentials above).

sections.title: Budget & daily costs
sections.intro: Solo traveler, Comfortable tier ($150–300/day target from intake). Honest flag, done in the actual numbers below rather than a hedge: lodging + rental car alone run ≈$223/day — comfortably inside the target. Add food and it's ≈$288/day — still just under it. But add this trip's one splurge, the spa treatment, and the total for the week comes to ≈$1,963 over 6 days, or ≈$327/day — about 9% over the stated $300/day ceiling, not merely 'approaching' it. That's the honest tradeoff of the Day 2 spa pick, not a rounding error; the flights line below is also $0 (destination unknown), so a real home airport adds more on top. Figures below are sourced estimates (≈), not confirmed bookings.

items.label: Lodging, per night (Sedona, 3★ average)
items.note: ≈$173/night for a 3★ property; 2★ runs ≈$143, the town-wide all-hotel average is ≈$231 (pulled up by luxury resorts at $300–700+). Sedona is meaningfully pricier than a typical US leisure destination — shop early.

items.label: Rental car, per day (compact/mid-size)
items.note: ≈$39–44/day for economy/compact, ≈$57/day for a mid-size — recommended over rideshare for this trip (see Getting around).

items.label: Food & drink, per day
items.note: Own estimate, not a sourced figure — mixing a casual local breakfast/lunch spot with one nicer dinner.

items.label: Red Rock Pass (7-day)
items.note: Covers parking at Coconino National Forest fee sites (Bell Rock, Cathedral Rock trailhead, and others) for the whole week — cheaper than the $5/day rate past 3 uses.

items.label: Spa treatment (Day 2)
items.note: ⚠ Own estimate, not sourced — L'Auberge de Sedona's spa menu (Deep Tissue, Swedish, Desert Flower, 60/90 min) doesn't publish prices online. Call ahead to confirm before booking.

items.label: Flights — round trip
items.note: Omitted — depends entirely on your home airport; the intake didn't specify one.

sections.title: Health & pharmacy
sections.body:
These dates fall inside Arizona's monsoon season (mid-June through end of September) — treat this as a real safety topic, not a packing footnote. Afternoon thunderstorms can send flash floods through washes and canyons miles from where the storm actually hit, with no warning if you can't see the clouds. Six inches of fast-moving water can knock down an adult; 18 inches can carry away a vehicle. Practical rules: never hike during rain or an active storm forecast; never cross a running wash; finish outdoor plans by early afternoon before storms typically build; check the NWS forecast the morning of, not the night before.

⚠ Active wildfire closures, current as of this check: the Pocket Fire (started mid-June 2026, ≈7 miles north of Sedona) is largely contained but has triggered an official Coconino National Forest closure order (#03-04-00-26-004, effective Jul 13 – Sep 30 2026, unless rescinded early) covering Oak Creek Canyon–area trails and sites — including Devil's Bridge, West Fork Trail, Soldier Pass Trail, Boynton Canyon Trail, and the Call of the Canyon picnic site. Separately, a stretch of Oak Creek itself (Call of the Canyon/West Fork down toward Sedona) is closed for flooding as of Jul 18 — burned ground sheds rain fast, so treat this stretch as higher flash-flood risk than the general monsoon warning above, not lower. None of this touches Cathedral Rock, Bell Rock, or Airport Mesa (all south of town, outside the fire area) — this trip's main hiking days are unaffected. It does affect Day 6's Oak Creek Canyon drive — see that day for specifics. Re-check the Coconino National Forest's alerts page close to your dates, since closures can lift before their stated end date.

Elevation (≈4,350 ft) and low humidity mean dehydration sneaks up faster than it would at sea level — carry more water than feels necessary, especially on any trail. High-desert sun is intense; sunscreen, a hat, and shade breaks matter even on a “short walk.”

For anything more than minor: Verde Valley Medical Center in Cottonwood (≈20 min from Sedona) is the nearest full hospital; Sedona itself has an urgent care clinic in town.

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
Sedona's red rocks — including the sites marketed as “vortexes” — were sacred to the Yavapai and Hopi long before the New Age movement arrived. Treat them accordingly, whether or not you personally engage with the energy-site framing.

  • Stay on marked trails — off-trail travel damages fragile high-desert soil crust that takes decades to recover.
  • Don't build rock cairns or stack rocks for photos — existing rock features may carry cultural or wayfinding significance, and moving them disrupts both.
  • Leave no trace — carry out all trash, including fruit peels and anything else that feels “natural.”
  • Keep voices low at vortex/viewpoint sites — you'll likely see people meditating, doing yoga, or holding quiet ceremony; treat it the way you'd treat any place of quiet reflection.
  • Don't leave offerings, ribbons, or crystals at sites — well-intentioned but treated as littering and cultural appropriation by both land managers and Native communities.
  • Support local, Native-owned businesses where you can — several galleries and shops in the area are Native-owned; ask, and prefer them over generic souvenir shops.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: PHX Airport → Sedona: rental car, ≈2 hours via I-17/AZ-179 (add ≈30 min on weekends). This is the recommended default for the whole trip — see below.

⚠ Sedona's free trailhead shuttle is only PARTIALLY running: Route 15 (Cathedral Rock/Little Horse) is active; Routes 11 (Dry Creek/Mescal) and 14 (Soldier Pass) are suspended because of the Pocket Fire closure (Forest Order #03-04-00-26-004, in effect through Sep 30 2026 unless rescinded early — see Health & safety). Since the order covers your whole trip window, plan around both routes staying down; re-check sedonashuttle.com and the Coconino NF alerts page close to your dates only to see if it lifted early.
Sedona Shuttle Connect: on-demand curb-to-curb service, ≈$2/ride, where it's running — a backup option, not the primary plan, given the route suspensions above.

Rideshare (Uber/Lyft): available but reported inconsistent, especially in peak season, with drivers often based out of Flagstaff (≈30 min away) rather than Sedona itself. Don't plan around it being available on demand.
Around town (Uptown, Tlaquepaque): walkable once parked — most of Days 4 and 5 need no driving between stops once you've arrived.
Any trailhead parking (Bell Rock, Cathedral Rock, Airport Mesa's lower lot): requires the Red Rock Pass. Airport Mesa's rim pullouts are a free alternative with very limited spots — arrive early.

items.title: Arrival — the long way in
items.pace: Slow — travel day, nothing else planned
items.tldr: Fly into PHX, drive ≈2 hours to Sedona, and let sunset at Airport Mesa be the only plan for the evening.
items.body: Land at PHX, pick up the rental car, drive ≈2 hours north to Sedona (I-17 → AZ-179). Check in, unpack at your own pace. As the light turns gold, head to Airport Mesa's free rim pullouts for sunset — arrive 30–45 minutes early, since the small free lots fill up and the paid $3 upper lot is the backup, not the plan. No dinner reservation needed tonight; keep it easy after a long travel day.
constraints: Arrive at Airport Mesa 30-45 min before sunset — free lots fill fast

items.title: Spa day
items.pace: Slow — one booking, the rest of the day open
items.tldr: A full spa morning at L'Auberge de Sedona, then the day stays deliberately unscheduled.
items.body:
L'Auberge de Sedona's L'Apothecary Spa, set along Oak Creek — Deep Tissue, Swedish, or Desert Flower massage, 60 or 90 minutes. Prices aren't published online; ⚠ call ahead to book and confirm cost. Mii amo, Sedona's best-known destination spa (built directly on one of the vortex sites), doesn't do single-day treatments at all — their site structures every stay as an all-inclusive "Journey": 2, 3, 4, 7, or 10 nights, each tied to specific arrival days (2-night Journeys start Sunday or Tuesday; 3-night, Thursday; 4-night, Sunday; 7-night, Thursday or Sunday; 10-night, Thursday). A 2-night Journey is the one that could actually fit inside this 6-night trip if it's worth restructuring a couple of days around it — everything else needs more time than this trip has. Otherwise, L'Auberge's single-treatment option is the realistic pick for a single spa morning. After your treatment: a slow lunch by the creek, afternoon left open on purpose.

Book? Call ahead for L'Auberge (855-905-5745) — no confirmed online same-day booking found. Mii amo takes Journey reservations at 844-993-9518; no day-pass or single-treatment option is offered to non-overnight guests.
constraints: Call ahead — no confirmed walk-in booking

items.title: Red rocks, the easy way
items.tldr: Cathedral Rock and Bell Rock from their easy, non-scramble vantage points — done by early afternoon before any monsoon storms build.
items.body:
Morning start, Red Rock Pass on the dashboard. Bell Rock Pathway is a mostly flat, easy walk with red-rock views the whole way — no scrambling required. Cathedral Rock's actual summit trail involves hand-over-hand rock scrambling and isn't the pick for a relaxed pace; view it instead from the pathway/vista areas, which need no climbing. Both sites are considered sacred by the Yavapai and Hopi — quiet voice, stay on trail, no cairns (see Etiquette). Finish outdoor time by early afternoon — September is inside monsoon season, and afternoon storms can trigger flash floods with no warning even from a clear sky overhead. Afternoon: back in town, rest or a slow lunch.

Where? Both trailheads sit along AZ-179, ≈10–15 min from Uptown. Book? Walk-in; just bring the Red Rock Pass.
constraints: Outdoor plans end by early afternoon — monsoon storm risk

items.title: Local food + Uptown
items.pace: Balanced — one neighborhood, mostly on foot
items.tldr: Breakfast at a local favorite, an afternoon browsing Uptown's galleries, dinner at Sedona's most in-demand kitchen.
items.body:
Breakfast at Wildcraft Cafe (health-conscious, organic, open daily 8am–3pm) or the 30-year local institution Coffee Pot Restaurant. Spend the afternoon browsing Uptown Sedona's galleries and shops on foot — lighter shopping than Day 5's dedicated stop, more about the wander. For dinner, Elote Cafe is Sedona's most in-demand table (bold Southwestern/Mexican flavors) — reservations recommended, not a walk-in bet on a Saturday. If it's booked out, El Rincon is a reliable, no-frills Mexican alternative.

Book? Elote Cafe — reserve ahead. Everything else is walk-in.
constraints: Elote Cafe — reserve ahead, especially for a Saturday

items.title: Tlaquepaque
items.pace: Slow — one destination, all day if you want it
items.tldr: Sedona's arts-and-shopping village — Spanish colonial architecture, galleries, courtyards, and Sunday's slightly longer hours.
items.body:
Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village — Spanish colonial-style courtyards built around sycamore trees, galleries, and boutiques rather than chain shopping. Hours run Mon–Thu 10am–5pm, Fri–Sun 10am–6pm — Sunday's extended hours make this a good full-day pick. Lunch on-site; several restaurants are tucked into the courtyards.

How from base? A short drive or walk from Uptown, ≈5–10 min. Book? Walk-in for the village itself.

items.title: Scenic drive + last relaxation
items.pace: Slow — a drive, not a hike, and nothing after
items.tldr: Oak Creek Canyon's scenic byway in the morning — check the current wildfire-closure status first — then a deliberately empty final afternoon and evening.
items.body: ⚠ Check before you go: a Pocket Fire closure order (through Sep 30 2026 — see Health & safety) closes several Oak Creek Canyon trails and picnic sites, and a separate flooding closure affects part of the creek itself. Driving AZ-89A through the canyon looks to still be allowed under a transit-only exemption, but stopping at pull-offs inside the closure area may not be — the order doesn't spell out every pull-off individually. Check the Coconino NF alerts page the morning of; if pull-offs are open, take the slow drive up the canyon and back, matching the pace the rest of this trip has kept. If they're not, an easy fallback that's confirmed unaffected: a slower return to Bell Rock or Airport Mesa (both south of town, outside the fire area) for the views without the Friday hike, or a last unhurried wander back through Uptown. Either way, be back in Sedona by early afternoon (same monsoon-timing logic as Friday). The rest of the day is intentionally unplanned: a last easy walk, packing at a relaxed pace, an early dinner wherever felt good earlier in the week.
constraints: Verify Oak Creek Canyon pull-off access before relying on it — active closure order through Sep 30
Back from whichever option by early afternoon — monsoon storm risk

items.title: Departure
items.tldr: A light final morning, then the ≈2-hour drive back to PHX — budget real time for it, not just the flight.
items.body: Pack, a light breakfast. Unlike a direct-arrival destination, getting to the airport here IS a significant part of the day — budget the full ≈2 hours back to PHX (more with weekend traffic) plus normal airport time, not just a short transfer.

items.name: Cathedral Rock
items.body: Sedona's most photographed formation. The actual summit trail requires hand-over-hand rock scrambling — not a fit for a relaxed pace or a first solo trip. The pathway and vista viewpoints deliver the same red-rock views without the climb. Considered sacred by the Yavapai and Hopi; stay on trail, keep voices low.

items.name: Bell Rock
items.body: A mostly flat pathway with constant red-rock views and none of Cathedral Rock's scramble — the better pick for an easy morning. Requires the Red Rock Pass for trailhead parking.

items.name: Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village
items.kicker: Spanish colonial courtyards, not a mall
items.body: Built around existing sycamore trees rather than clearing them — galleries, boutiques, and restaurants inside genuinely distinctive architecture. Fri–Sun keeps later hours (10am–6pm) than the Mon–Thu schedule (10am–5pm).

img.alt: Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village courtyard in Sedona

items.name: Airport Mesa Viewpoint
items.kicker: Free sunset views, if you're early
items.body: 360-degree red-rock views from a rim pulled right off Airport Road. The rim pullouts are free but hold only a few cars each — arrive 30-45 minutes before sunset. A paid $3 upper lot is the fallback if the free spots are gone.

img.alt: Sunset view from Airport Mesa near Sedona

sections.title: What to eat
sections.body:
Wildcraft Cafe — health-conscious, organic, vegan/paleo/gluten-free options, open daily 8am–3pm — is the pick for an easy local breakfast or lunch that isn't a hotel buffet.

Elote Cafe is Sedona's most in-demand table: bold, elevated Southwestern and Mexican cooking. Reserve ahead, especially on a weekend — it's not a reliable walk-in.

El Rincon and Tamaliza both do traditional Mexican food without the elevated price or the wait — good backups if Elote is booked out.

Coffee Pot Restaurant has been a west-side local institution for 30+ years — a straightforward, unpretentious breakfast spot.

A note on tourist-trap avoidance: the generic “best restaurants in Sedona” lists skew toward the Uptown strip closest to hotels — this guide deliberately routes through spots local guides actually name as where residents eat.

sections.title: Sources & further reading
sections.body:

  • Coconino National Forest — Red Rock Pass Program
  • Coconino National Forest — current alerts & closures (Pocket Fire)
  • Sedona Shuttle — live route status
  • National Weather Service Flagstaff — Flash Flood safety
  • Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village — official site
  • L'Auberge de Sedona — L'Apothecary Spa
  • Mii amo — destination spa
